_meta.description: Key financials for all 38 GSE-listed companies. Figures are approximate, sourced from training knowledge based on latest available annual reports (FY2022–FY2023). Revenue and profit in GHS millions unless currency noted. Update annually.
_meta.note: Ghana DDEP (Domestic Debt Exchange Programme, Feb 2023) caused one-time bond impairment losses across all banks. FY2022 bank results are depressed; FY2023 shows recovery. Cross-listed stocks (ETI, TLW, AGA, GLD) report in USD.

keyStrengths: Largest telecom in Ghana with ~53% mobile market share
Strong data revenue growth (30%+ YoY) driven by mobile internet
MTN Group backing — capital, technology and brand support
Fintech arm (MoMo) becoming a significant revenue contributor
keyRisks: Currency devaluation increases USD-denominated costs (roaming, equipment)
Regulatory risk from NCA (National Communications Authority)
Competition from Telecel (formerly Vodafone Ghana) and AirtelTigo
MTNGH.GH.analystNote: Blue-chip GSE stock. Consistent dividend payer with semi-annual distributions. Largest company by market cap on GSE. Benefits from Ghana's youthful, mobile-first population.

keyStrengths: Ghana's largest indigenous bank with 60+ years of history
Extensive branch and ATM network across Ghana
Strong retail and SME banking franchise
Government-related entity — perceived implicit backing
keyRisks: High NPL (non-performing loan) ratio typical of Ghanaian banks
DDEP (2023) impaired government bond portfolio — ongoing recovery
Currency risk on USD-denominated liabilities
Public sector salary payment dependency makes it exposed to govt delays
GCB.GH.analystNote: Ghana's flagship bank. DDEP caused significant bond losses in FY2022; recovery visible in FY2023. Solid dividend payer. P/E historically 5–10x. Well-known brand but capital quality concerns linger post-DDEP.

keyStrengths: Recovery trajectory after severe DDEP bond write-offs in 2022
Strong digital banking platform (Cal Mobile)
Growing SME and trade finance book
keyRisks: Heavily impacted by DDEP — recorded large losses in FY2022
Capital adequacy under pressure; may need capital raise
Dividend suspended during recovery
High NPL ratio and concentration risk
CAL.GH.analystNote: Hardest-hit bank from DDEP after GN Bank collapse-related issues. FY2022 recorded net loss; FY2023 is a recovery year but still weak. Higher risk, speculative at current stage. Dividend suspended.

keyStrengths: Backed by Ecobank Transnational — pan-African brand and capital
Strong trade finance and corporate banking capability
Well-capitalised relative to local peers
keyRisks: DDEP impact on GoG bond portfolio (2022 losses)
Parent (ETI) has had its own capital pressures
Currency risk on USD liabilities
EGH.GH.analystNote: One of the better-managed banks in Ghana. ETI parentage provides international credibility. Moderate dividend. More conservative lending than local banks historically.

keyStrengths: International bank with Standard Chartered Group backing and standards
Conservative lending with lower NPL ratios than local peers
Strong corporate and trade finance franchise
Best-in-class capital adequacy ratios
keyRisks: Limited retail banking presence vs. GCB and Ecobank
DDEP impact (lower than local banks due to lower GoG bond exposure)
StanChart Group strategic repositioning could affect Ghana focus
SCB.GH.analystNote: Highest-quality bank on GSE by lending standards. Lower risk than local banks. Good dividend payer. Tends to trade at a premium P/E to reflect quality. Less GSE liquidity than GCB/EGH.

keyStrengths: Preference share — fixed preferential dividend ahead of ordinary shareholders
Standard Chartered Bank Ghana backing — low default risk
Income security higher than ordinary equity
keyRisks: No capital appreciation (fixed-income like instrument)
Dividend could be suspended if bank faces distress
Interest rate risk — value falls if market rates rise
SCBPREF.GH.analystNote: This is a preference share, not ordinary equity. Suitable for income-focused investors only. Acts more like a fixed-income instrument. Not suitable for capital growth.

keyStrengths: Backed by Access Bank Nigeria (one of Africa's largest banks)
Growing rapidly through acquisition strategy
Strong trade finance and diaspora banking capabilities
keyRisks: Parent (Access Bank Nigeria) managing its own large acquisition debt
DDEP impact on local bond portfolio
Rapid growth can strain capital adequacy
ACCESS.GH.analystNote: Nigerian parent brings capital and pan-African ambitions. Growth-oriented but dividend is modest. Watch capital ratios as loan book expands fast. Medium risk.

keyStrengths: SocGen (France) parent provides international credibility
Corporate banking focus with quality clients
keyRisks: SocGen Group has been divesting African assets — strategic uncertainty
Small market share; DDEP impacted bond portfolio
Low liquidity on GSE
SOGEGH.GH.analystNote: SocGen global has been reducing African footprint. Ghana unit faces strategic uncertainty as parent may divest. Low trading volume stock. Moderate risk.

keyStrengths: Government development mandate — agriculture sector focus
Large branch network in rural areas
Policy support from government
keyRisks: High NPL ratio (agriculture lending is riskier)
Government ownership can lead to political lending decisions
DDEP impact + structural capital weakness
Low profitability — ROE below cost of capital
ADB.GH.analystNote: Development bank with a mandate, not a return-maximising commercial bank. Low return on equity historically. Not suitable for growth investors. Income potential limited.

keyStrengths: Growing loan book in SME and retail segments
Digital banking investments improving efficiency
keyRisks: Smaller balance sheet limits large corporate deals
DDEP bond impairments in FY2022
Low liquidity on GSE
FAB.GH.analystNote: Mid-tier bank with growth ambitions. Improving profitability trend post-DDEP. Limited GSE trading liquidity. Medium risk.

keyStrengths: Backed by Republic Financial Holdings (Trinidad) — Caribbean banking expertise
Focus on mortgage and home finance (formerly HFC Bank)
keyRisks: Small market share in competitive Ghanaian banking sector
Mortgage lending exposed to Ghanaian property market softness
DDEP impact (2022)
RBGH.GH.analystNote: Formerly HFC Bank, rebranded after Republic Financial Holdings acquisition. Mortgage focus differentiates it slightly. Low GSE liquidity. Low-medium risk.

keyStrengths: Libyan Arab African Investment Company backing (foreign capital)
keyRisks: Smallest bank on GSE — very low market share
Minimal trading volume, very illiquid
No dividend history
Geopolitical risk from Libyan parent
TBL.GH.analystNote: Very small bank with minimal GSE presence. Extremely illiquid. Primarily held by founding shareholders. Not recommended for retail investors seeking liquidity.

keyStrengths: Pan-African presence across 35 countries — unique diversification
Leading pan-African digital banking and payments platform (Omni Plus)
Strong trade finance network connecting African economies
keyRisks: Exposure to multiple African currency devaluations simultaneously
Capital pressure from large balance sheet across fragile economies
Complex holding structure makes analysis difficult
Operating in some high-risk jurisdictions (West/Central Africa)
ETI.GH.analystNote: Financials in USD. Pan-African banking group listed on Ghana, Nigeria and Côte d'Ivoire exchanges. Recovery post-DDEP Ghana and various country restructurings. Diversification is both strength and complexity. Medium-risk.

keyStrengths: Ghana's largest insurance conglomerate (Enterprise Life + Enterprise Insurance)
Diversified into life insurance, general insurance and fund management
Strong brand and distribution network across Ghana
Investment portfolio benefits from rising interest rates
keyRisks: Insurance penetration in Ghana is low — growth requires market education
Investment returns linked to Ghanaian sovereign bond yields (DDEP impact)
Currency risk on USD-denominated reinsurance premiums
EGL.GH.analystNote: Best-in-class insurance company on GSE. Consistent dividend payer. Well-managed with strong brand. Benefits from rising interest rates on investment portfolio. Relatively defensive stock. Low-medium risk.

keyStrengths: Government ownership provides franchise security
Large pool of existing policyholders
keyRisks: State-owned — prone to political/bureaucratic decision-making
Low profitability vs. private sector peer Enterprise Group
Claims ratio management challenges
Has had years of losses or near-breakeven results
SIC.GH.analystNote: Government-owned insurer — less commercially focused than EGL. Often loss-making or marginal. Not recommended unless at very distressed valuation. EGL is the clear preferred insurance pick on GSE.

keyStrengths: Wilmar International subsidiary — world's largest agribusiness conglomerate backing
Prime agricultural land in Wenchi (Brong-Ahafo region)
Self-sufficient in power (own hydro + biomass generation)
Consistent dividend payer with strong free cash flow
keyRisks: Commodity price risk — crude palm oil (CPO) price fluctuations
Climate/weather risk on crop yields
Wilmar could delist or buy out minority shareholders
BOPP.GH.analystNote: One of the best fundamentals on GSE. Strong cash flow, low debt, consistent dividends, and a world-class parent. Oil palm is a defensive commodity with growing global demand. Recommended for income + moderate growth investors.

keyStrengths: Strategic asset — processes Ghana's key export commodity (cocoa)
Government support likely given national importance
keyRisks: Chronically loss-making — high energy and processing costs
High leverage (Debt/Equity >2x) — financial distress risk
Government ownership leads to inefficiency
Volatile cocoa bean prices compress margins
Current ratio below 1 — liquidity concern
CPC.GH.analystNote: One of the most consistently loss-making companies on GSE. High debt, no dividend, negative ROE. Speculative only — potential turnaround or privatisation play. Avoid for regular investment.

keyStrengths: Iconic consumer brands (OMO, Sunlight, Lipton, Close-Up) with strong recall
Wide distribution network across Ghana
Post-Unilever-divestiture management focused on local optimization
keyRisks: Unilever divested majority stake — may lose brand licenses and supply chain support over time
Margin compression from forex-linked input costs (palm oil, chemicals)
Intense competition from importers and local brands
Net margins very thin (~3%) — vulnerable to cost shocks
UNIL.GH.analystNote: Unilever Global divested its majority Ghana stake (now locally controlled). Transition creates uncertainty around brand licenses. Thin margins, inconsistent dividend. Household brand recognition is the main moat. Medium risk.

keyStrengths: Market leader in frozen dairy (ice cream, Fan Ice, Fan Milk) in Ghana
Strong brand loyalty across all income segments
Danone/international backing provides product development support
keyRisks: Margins compressed by rising electricity costs (cold chain critical)
Forex impact on milk powder and packaging imports
Danone Group has been divesting brands globally — divestiture risk
FML.GH.analystNote: Fan Milk brand is a Ghanaian household name. Steady margins, consistent dividends, but growth is slow. Danone ownership change could affect strategy. Defensive FMCG stock. Low-medium risk.

keyStrengths: Diageo brand portfolio (Guinness, Malta Guinness, Smirnoff, Baileys)
Strong distribution reach across Ghana
Premium and popular segment presence
keyRisks: Excise duty increases squeeze margins on beer/spirits
Consumer purchasing power constrained by inflation
Competition from cheaper imports and local brewers (AB InBev Ghana)
Forex-linked raw materials (malt, hops) erode margins
GGBL.GH.analystNote: Diageo subsidiary with powerful brands but thin margins. Has had loss-making years. Consumer spending pressure in Ghana hits discretionary alcohol. Not the safest GSE pick. Speculative to moderate risk.

keyStrengths: Niche printing and stationery business with established clients
keyRisks: Extremely small company — very low trading volume on GSE
Digitisation reducing demand for printed materials
No dividend, minimal growth
Illiquid — difficult to buy or sell
CMLT.GH.analystNote: Micro-cap printing company with extremely low trading volume. Not suitable for most investors. Very limited growth prospects in an increasingly digital world.

keyStrengths: Local food brand with distribution in key Ghanaian markets
keyRisks: Very small company with limited financial data available
Extremely illiquid on GSE
Intense FMCG competition from larger players
No dividend history
SAMBA.GH.analystNote: Small food company with very limited publicly available financial data. Extremely illiquid on GSE. High uncertainty — analysis is speculative.

keyStrengths: Merger of Dannex and Ayrton Drug (two established Ghanaian pharma companies)
Manufacturing capability for generics — import substitution play
Growing demand for affordable medicines in Ghana
keyRisks: Import competition from cheap Indian and Chinese generics
Forex exposure on active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs)
Regulatory compliance costs for GMP standards
Limited R&D capability — pure generics manufacturer
DASPHARMA.GH.analystNote: Ghana's main domestic pharmaceutical manufacturer. Healthcare is a secular growth theme. Margins thin due to import competition but growing local manufacturing drive helps. Medium risk, long-term structural opportunity.

keyStrengths: Niche manufacturer of IV fluids — essential healthcare product
Relatively low competition domestically for IV manufacturing
Healthcare is a non-cyclical sector
keyRisks: Very small company — limited scale and reinvestment capacity
Dependent on public sector (Ministry of Health, hospitals) procurement
Government procurement delays create cash flow issues
Very low GSE trading volume
IIL.GH.analystNote: Niche IV fluid manufacturer serving Ghana's healthcare sector. Non-cyclical product but heavy government procurement dependence and very small size. Very illiquid on GSE.

keyStrengths: Ghana's largest indigenous petroleum marketing company (GOIL = Ghana Oil)
Largest retail fuel station network in Ghana (600+ stations nationwide)
Government strategic importance — unlikely to be disrupted
Growing LPG and aviation fuel segments
keyRisks: Razor-thin margins typical of fuel retail (~2% net margin)
Petroleum price regulation by NPA can squeeze margins
Forex exposure on crude oil import costs
EV transition (very long-term risk in Ghana context)
GOIL.GH.analystNote: Revenue is large but margins are thin — typical fuel retail dynamics. Strong brand and network. National strategic importance. Consistent dividend. Good for income investors. Medium-low risk.

keyStrengths: TotalEnergies (French major) parent — global best practices and capital
Premium positioning (higher margin lubricants and specialty fuels)
Strong corporate and aviation fuel contracts
keyRisks: Global parent TotalEnergies could delist from GSE
Thin fuel retail margins; NPA price regulation
Forex exposure on crude imports
TOTAL.GH.analystNote: TotalEnergies subsidiary with slightly better margins than GOIL due to higher-value product mix (lubricants, aviation). Consistent dividend, international backing. Premium to GOIL on P/E justified. Low-medium risk.

keyStrengths: Growing fuel retail footprint in Ghana
keyRisks: No dividend, very thin margins
Current ratio below 1 — liquidity pressure
Higher leverage than GOIL/TOTAL
Limited financial history as a listed company
Very low GSE trading volume
ZEN.GH.analystNote: Smaller fuel retailer vs GOIL and Total. Thinner margins, higher debt, no dividend, and liquidity concerns. Would choose GOIL or TOTAL over ZEN for fuel sector exposure. High risk.

keyStrengths: Operator of Ghana's Jubilee and TEN offshore oil fields
Oil production is cash-generative at Brent above ~$50/bbl
West Africa portfolio with long-dated production licenses
keyRisks: Heavy debt load (~$1.7B net debt) — interest costs consume most operating profit
Declining production rates from maturing Ghanaian fields
Oil price sensitivity — highly leveraged to Brent price
Dividend suspended since 2020; reinstatement uncertain
Ongoing asset divestiture programme
TLW.GH.analystNote: USD-denominated. Highly leveraged oil company facing declining Ghana production. Speculative — only attractive if oil prices spike and debt is reduced. Dividend suspended. HIGH risk investment.

keyStrengths: Tier 1 global gold miner with assets across Africa, Americas and Australia
Moved primary listing to NYSE — improved global institutional access
Benefits directly from rising gold price (safe-haven demand)
Strong balance sheet with manageable debt
keyRisks: Gold price sensitivity — earnings fall sharply with gold price decline
Operational risk in high-risk jurisdictions (DRC, Mali, Guinea)
Rising energy and labour costs at mines
Ghana GSE cross-listing is secondary — limited local trading depth
AGA.GH.analystNote: USD-denominated. AngloGold is a genuine global gold major. Buy if you want gold price exposure with a diversified mining company. GSE listing provides Ghanaian investors access. Tracks NYSE primary listing. Medium risk (gold price risk).

keyStrengths: Owner and operator of Bibiani Gold Mine in Ghana's Western Region — producing asset
Growing gold production profile since 2022 acquisition from Resolute Mining
Listed on CSE (Canada) and GSE — dual market visibility
Leverage to gold price upside with Ghana operational base
keyRisks: Junior miner — high capital expenditure during ramp-up, FCF negative
Balance sheet leverage from mine acquisition financing
Operational execution risk at Bibiani — relatively new operator
USD reporting currency — GHS investors face FX exposure
ASG.GH.analystNote: Asante Gold Corporation (ASG) is a Canadian gold mining company that acquired the Bibiani Gold Mine in Ghana in 2022. Changed fiscal year from January 31 to December 31. Growth-oriented junior miner in ramp-up phase. Not a dividend payer. Higher risk/reward than established Ghanaian miners.

keyStrengths: Depositary share exposure to AngloGold Ashanti global operations
keyRisks: Depositary shares add a layer of complexity over direct shares
Very low liquidity on GSE
Tracking error vs. primary NYSE listing
AADS.GH.analystNote: American Depositary Shares of AngloGold Ashanti listed on GSE. Complex instrument. For local exposure to AGA, prefer AGA.GH directly. Very thin market.

keyStrengths: Gold Fields operates Tarkwa (Ghana) and Damang — two world-class mines
Strong balance sheet — net debt declining year-on-year
Better cost discipline than AGA historically (lower AISC)
South African primary listing with global investor base
keyRisks: Gold price sensitivity — highly correlated with Brent/USD/safe-haven demand
Mine life — Tarkwa transitioning to deeper underground (higher costs)
South Africa JSE primary listing — currency conversion complexity for GHS investors
Global climate regulation increasing mining compliance costs
GLD.GH.analystNote: USD-denominated. Gold Fields GDR gives exposure to one of Africa's best-managed gold miners. Tarkwa mine in Ghana is a flagship asset. Generally better cost profile than AGA. Recommended for gold sector exposure. Medium risk.

keyStrengths: IT services in a growing digital economy
keyRisks: Micro-cap with essentially zero trading volume
No dividend, minimal profits
Competition from large global tech companies entering Ghana
Very limited financial disclosure
CLYD.GH.analystNote: Near-dormant micro-cap IT company. Essentially untradeable due to zero market volume. No financial rationale for investment at current stage. Avoid.

keyStrengths: Digital media/signage niche — some recurring revenue from contracts
keyRisks: Extremely small, essentially illiquid
No dividend, very low earnings
Limited competitive differentiation
DIGICUT.GH.analystNote: Micro-cap digital media company. Essentially untradeable. No investment case for most investors.

keyStrengths: Holds Ewoyaa Lithium Project — one of Africa's highest-grade hard-rock lithium deposits
Ghana government partnership (GNPC) and offtake agreements with major battery producers
Listed on ASX (ALL), AIM/LSE and GSE — broad international investor access
Strategic lithium asset in mining-friendly Ghana jurisdiction
keyRisks: Pre-production exploration company — zero revenue, negative cash flow
Corporate restructuring underway (Elevra/Zhejiang transfer discussions)
Lithium price collapse (2023-2025) has severely depressed developer valuations
Long timeline to production — capex, permitting, and offtake risks
AUD-reporting currency creates FX complexity for GHS investors
ALLGH.GH.analystNote: Atlantic Lithium Limited (formerly listed as ALLGH on GSE after Aluworks was suspended) is an Australian exploration company developing the Ewoyaa Lithium Project in Ghana. Pre-revenue and exploration-stage — high speculative risk. Formerly known as African Pioneer Ltd. Complex corporate changes underway. Not suitable for income or value investors.

keyStrengths: Established distribution business with government and corporate clients
Low debt relative to size
keyRisks: Small scale — limited pricing power
Very low GSE trading volume
Automotive distribution business exposed to import restrictions
Revenue largely depends on government procurement cycles
MAC.GH.analystNote: Small distribution company with some dividend history. Very illiquid on GSE. Limited growth prospects. Not recommended for most investors.

keyStrengths: Diversified distribution holding company
Some recurring revenues from service contracts
keyRisks: Low liquidity on GSE
Small scale limits reinvestment
Conglomerate complexity with limited transparency
MMH.GH.analystNote: Small distribution conglomerate. Limited public financial information. Very illiquid. Not recommended.

keyStrengths: Real estate assets in Ghana are structurally valuable long-term
Rental income provides some revenue stability
keyRisks: Very small real estate company — minimal scale
Extremely low GSE trading volume
Ghana real estate market hit by currency devaluation and high mortgage rates
No dividend, limited financial disclosure
HORDS.GH.analystNote: Micro-cap real estate company with very limited GSE presence. Essentially untradeable. No investment case for most investors at this stage.
